content.text: After a tool executes, its output must be packaged into a

block and sent back to the model in the next user message. How you format, truncate, and error-handle the result determines what the model knows and how it responds. Getting this right is the difference between an agent that gracefully adapts and one that hallucinates or crashes.

attrs.title: Success → json.dumps, Error → is_error: True — both produce a tool_result block

content.text: The model cannot see your application's state — it only sees what you put in the tool_result.
An incomplete, truncated, or poorly formatted result is indistinguishable from the real answer, so the model will reason from it as if it were complete. Be explicit about what is missing or failed.
block must be a string. Dicts, lists, numbers, and booleans must all be serialised with
Model can read JSON fields and refer to them by key
Plain text works; JSON is more structured
Arithmetic values need no special handling
Signal absence explicitly rather than omitting content

Tool results enter the conversation context and remain there for all subsequent turns. A 50 KB document returned as-is will inflate every future API call. Truncate or summarise large results at the tool boundary:

# Tool results that are very large slow down subsequent model calls
# because they are added to the conversation context.
# Truncate or summarise before returning.

MAX_RESULT_CHARS = 4000

def get_document(doc_id: str) -> dict:
    doc = storage.fetch(doc_id)
    body = doc["body"]
    truncated = len(body) > MAX_RESULT_CHARS
    return {
        "title": doc["title"],
        "body": body[:MAX_RESULT_CHARS],
        "truncated": truncated,
        "total_chars": len(body),
        "hint": "Use get_document_section to fetch a specific section." if truncated else None
    }

def search_results(query: str, max_results: int = 5) -> dict:
    hits = search_engine.query(query, limit=50)
    # Return only the top N results, not all 50
    return {
        "query": query,
        "total_found": len(hits),
        "results": [{"title": h.title, "snippet": h.snippet[:200]} for h in hits[:max_results]],
        "note": f"Showing {min(max_results, len(hits))} of {len(hits)} results."
    }
Set a MAX_RESULT_CHARS budget (4000–8000 chars is typical for a single result)
Always include a 'truncated: true' flag so the model knows the result is partial
Add a 'hint' field suggesting how to fetch the rest (e.g. a get_document_section tool)
For search results, return top-N with total_found so the model knows what was omitted
field can be a list of content blocks instead of a string. This lets a tool return both text and images in the same result — useful for browser automation, chart generation, or any visual output:
The image block uses the same
string. Only use multi-content results when the image is genuinely informative — it adds context-window cost.
Returning errors as plain strings works but gives the model little to act on. A structured error with a machine-readable code and a
flag lets the model explain the error accurately and decide whether to try again:
Reports the missing item to the user
Informs user of the access restriction
Reports the bad argument, may ask user to clarify
May retry after a pause or inform the user
Retries or asks user to try again later
Retries once or reports the slowness
blocks can fill the context window. Compress large results after they have been used by replacing them with shortened summaries:

before each API call when your agent runs more than 5–10 tool turns. This is especially important for agents that call document-fetching or search tools repeatedly — without compression, context cost grows linearly with the number of turns.
Tool results are read by the model as data, but you can embed a

field to guide the agent's next step. Hints communicate constraints that the description cannot predict at definition time — whether confirmation is required, whether the result is partial, or whether a follow-up tool is needed:

as part of the JSON result and incorporates it into its reasoning. Prefix the field with an underscore to distinguish it from real domain data. Use hints sparingly — they are most useful for recoverable conditions that the model cannot detect from the data alone.

Calling the same read-only tool with identical arguments multiple times within a run wastes latency and API quota. An in-memory cache with a TTL prevents redundant executions while keeping results fresh:
Only cache idempotent, read-only tools — never cache mutation tools (create, update, delete)
Set TTL based on how stale the data can be (60s for exchange rates, 300s for product prices, 0 for user-submitted data)
Include the tool name in the cache key so different tools never collide
Sort input keys before hashing to treat {a:1, b:2} and {b:2, a:1} as the same call

Tracking what tools were called, how long they took, and how often they errored is essential for debugging agent behaviour and catching regressions. A lightweight dataclass accumulator captures this during the loop without requiring an external service:
output gives you error rate, total calls, and the slowest tool at a glance. In production, emit this summary to your logging pipeline at the end of each run so you can trend it over time.
Handling tool results correctly requires three things: serialising all non-string values with
with a descriptive message when the tool fails; and truncating large results before they enter the context. For richer output,
can be a list of text and image blocks. Structured error objects with machine-readable codes and a
flag give the model better signals for recovery. In long-running agents, compress accumulated results between turns to prevent context-window exhaustion.
